planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.5–7.0), fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; allow ample space — D.D. Blanchard grows into a large, pyramidal tree 30–50 feet tall.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; D.D. Blanchard is one of the most magnificent Southern magnolia cultivars — distinguished by exceptionally dark, glossy, almost black-green upper leaf surfaces with a rich, coppery-orange to russet-brown underside that is among the most vivid and attractive of any Southern magnolia selection. The underside color is a significant ornamental feature: when a breeze stirs the branches, the leaves momentarily reveal their orange-bronze undersides, creating a shimmering two-tone effect that no other tree quite replicates. D.D. Blanchard is a large tree with a naturally pyramidal form, growing 30–50 feet tall with a 20–30 foot spread — a specimen tree with an imposing, stately presence appropriate for large residential landscapes and commercial settings. The giant, creamy-white, intensely fragrant flowers (8–12 inches across) bloom from May through July in zone 9, with intermittent bloom through summer. Like all Southern magnolia cultivars, D.D. Blanchard produces large, cone-like fruits with brilliant red seeds that are consumed by birds and wildlife. An outstanding selection for the Gulf South where the tree is most at home — performing magnificently in zone 8–10 heat and humidity.

common_name: Edith Bogue Magnolia (Cold-Hardy Upright Southern Magnolia)

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped trees in late winter or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Edith Bogue has a narrower, more upright form than most Southern magnolia cultivars — a significant advantage in smaller spaces.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Edith Bogue is the most cold-hardy Southern magnolia cultivar available for routine landscape use — surviving temperatures as low as -10°F and reliably winter-hardy in zone 5 where no other Southern magnolia cultivar can be grown. The cultivar originated from a tree planted by Edith Bogue in Montclair, New Jersey, that survived severe winters that killed all other Southern magnolia selections in the area. This exceptional cold hardiness expands the range of the magnificent Southern magnolia northward into climate zones previously inaccessible to the species. Beyond cold hardiness, Edith Bogue has a distinctly narrower, more columnar to pyramidal habit than most Southern magnolia cultivars — a significant advantage in residential landscapes where the typical broad, spreading form of standard Southern magnolias would require too much space. The upright form makes Edith Bogue appropriate for tighter spaces along buildings, driveways, and in urban settings. The glossy, dark-green leaves with a lighter underside and the classic creamy-white, deeply fragrant flowers (8–12 inches across) are characteristic of the species. In zone 9, it performs superbly — the superior cold hardiness is not needed in the Gulf South, but the narrow habit is a consistent advantage.

common_name: Royal Star Magnolia (Large White Star Magnolia)

planting.note: Plant container or balled-and-burlapped plants in late winter or very early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.5–7.0), fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; the later-blooming nature of Royal Star relative to other star magnolias reduces the risk of frost damage to the open flowers.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Royal Star is the premier star magnolia cultivar — selected for flowers significantly larger and with more petals (25–30 tepals per flower) than the standard Magnolia stellata species (12–18 tepals), creating a fuller, more sumptuous floral display in late winter to early spring. The flowers are pure white, intensely fragrant with a light vanilla-rose scent, and open from fuzzy silver-gray buds in late February through March (in zone 7–8) before any foliage appears — the bare branches covered with star-shaped, multi-petaled white flowers creating one of the most beautiful early spring floral spectacles of any woody plant. Royal Star also blooms approximately one week later than the species — a practical advantage since the open flowers of star magnolia are frequently damaged by late frosts in zone 4–7. The later bloom timing extends the frost-free blooming window significantly. Magnolia stellata and Royal Star are naturally slow-growing, multi-stemmed shrubs to small trees reaching 10–15 feet over many decades. In zone 9, star magnolia blooms very early but may struggle in the hottest, most humid portions — it performs best in zone 5–8 and in the cooler microclimates of zone 9a.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Gracillimus performs best in full sun — in partial shade the arching form becomes loose and floppy, and fall color and flowering are reduced.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Gracillimus is the classic maiden grass — the most refined and elegant Miscanthus sinensis cultivar, producing fine-textured, very narrow (1/4-inch wide), arching, medium-green leaves with a thin, white midrib that catches the sun and gives the planting a silver-glinting effect in breeze. The extremely fine leaf texture contrasts beautifully with bold-textured companions like hostas, rudbeckias, and coneflowers. Gracillimus grows into a large, gracefully arching fountain form (5–7 feet tall, 4–5 feet wide) that provides outstanding textural accent in the perennial border and a striking specimen effect when planted singly or in small groups. In late summer and fall, copper-red to bronze flower plumes rise above the foliage and dry to soft tan-gold, providing winter interest that persists until cut back in late winter. The seed heads are attractive to finches and other seed-eating birds. Note: Miscanthus sinensis can be invasive in some eastern states — check local invasive species guidance before planting near natural areas. In zone 9, Gracillimus is reliably perennial and may flower in late summer before cutting back in late winter. Cut to 6 inches in February before new growth emerges.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Strictus is more upright and stiffer than most miscanthus cultivars and does not require staking — a significant advantage in exposed positions where wind would topple arching types.

directions: Plant in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Strictus, also known as Porcupine Grass, is the most distinctive upright miscanthus cultivar — sharing the yellow banding characteristic of Zebrinus (zebra grass) but with an importantly different growth habit: while Zebrinus has a loose, arching, spreading form that can sprawl untidily, Strictus grows rigidly upright with a narrow, stiff, columnar form that stays tightly together and doesn't flop. The horizontal yellow bands that stripe each leaf perpendicular to its length create the unusual porcupine-stripe appearance that gives this cultivar both its common name and its striking visual effect — the banding is only visible at relatively close range but creates an unusual texture effect that is distinctive and unusual. The upright, narrow form (6–8 feet tall, 4 feet wide) makes Strictus outstanding for screening, privacy hedges, and vertical accent use where the wide-spreading form of other miscanthus types would be inappropriate. In fall, copper-bronze flower plumes rise above the foliage before drying to a persistent, ornamental winter structure. Like all Miscanthus sinensis cultivars, cut to 6 inches in late winter before new growth begins. Note invasive potential in some eastern states.
